data.title: Incyte: Buy For The Turnaround, Stay For The Pipeline
data.description:
Last February, I rated Incyte Corporation (INCY) a Strong Buy anticipating a turnaround for the stock. At the time, the market was fixated on the putative decline of the company's top-selling drug Jakafi following lackluster results in Q4 2024. Despite the lower than expected guidance, I urged readers to focus instead on Incyte's commercialization potential.

Since, the stock has appreciated by 56%, handily beating the 15% return of the S&P 500 (SP500). Two catalysts prompted this outperformance: first, Jakafi sales refuted predictions of a terminal decline, instead rising by 6.7% YoY in Q3 2025. Second, the launch of Opzelura internationally and in pediatric atopic dermatitis (AD) catapulted the drug to 35.0% YoY growth over the same time period. Taken together these two factors prompted Incyte's management to raise 2025 revenue guidance as Q3 sales increased by 20% YoY, beating the analyst consensus forecast by $120M or nearly 9%. The most impressive part of this result is that the ex-Jakafi business grew by nearly 45% YoY.

Following Q3, Incyte has clearly turned a corner. While the patent for Jakafi expires in 2028 and generic competition will ensue, the launch of an extended release should moderate the patent cliff. With ex-Jakafi growing so quickly analyst predictions for a major drop in 2029 profits and sales have begun to appear less likely. Furthermore, Incyte has seven pipeline candidates that were highlighted at this week's J.P. Morgan conference capable of reaching commercialization between 2027 and 2031, further offsetting the Jakafi patent cliff. Given the acceleration in Incyte's business, the time before generic competition begins and the additional pipeline catalysts that are coming; I continue to rate Incyte a Strong Buy, targeting $130.90/share or 22.5% appreciation from the current market price.

Incyte: Updates from Q3 2025 and the J.P. Morgan Conference

As of Q3 2025, the products sold by Incyte are shown in the table below. Jakafi still dominates the portfolio with $791.1M in sales, comprising 57.9% of revenues, which grew by 6.7% YoY. Jakafi was the first oral JAK1/2 inhibitor approved by the FDA as of 2011 for the treatment of adults with high-risk myelofibrosis (MF). Its label was then expanded over time to include: Polycythemiz Vera (PV), Acute and Chronic Graft-Versus-Host Disease (aGVHD & cGVHD). A major reason for the market's sentiment last year was the rejection of an extended release version of Jakafi in mid-2023. Jakafi is taken orally twice daily (BID), however, Incyte still anticipates an extended release once daily (QD) version to be launched by mid-2026. An extended release version will offer longer patent protection and is still a company focus to lessen the patent cliff in 2028. It should be noted that royalties on Jakafi sales outside the U.S. were $125.6M an additional 9.2% of revenues.

Next, Opzelura sales increased by 35.0% to $188M comprising 13.8% of the portfolio. Opzelura (ruxolitinib) is a novel cream formulation of the same active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) as Jakafi. It is approved by the FDA for use in mild to moderate AD.

After Opzelura, the ex-Jakafi business comprised of: Niktimvo (3.35% of sales), Monjuvi (3.07% of sales at 33.8% YoY growth), Zynyz (1.66% of sales), Pemazyre (1.66% of sales at 9.7% YoY growth) and Iclusig (2.75% of sales at 26.6% YoY growth). While these products remain small relative to Jakafi, combined they contributed $1.26B at a 45% YoY growth rate. The midpoint of 2025 revenue guidance was $4.275B, thus the growth portfolio now comprises 29.5% of Incyte's sales.

Management sees ex-Jakafi revenue tripling by 2030 to between $3B and $4B. If we take the low end of this guidance ($3B), this represents a CAGR of 19.0% for the ex-Jakafi portfolio. At the upper end ($4B), this represents a CAGR of 26.0% from now until 2030. This is essential, because this growth is what is needed to preemptively fill the patent cliff by 2028.

There are three years for the ex-Jakafi portfolio to grow before the patent cliff hits. The table below takes the CAGR implied by the low and high ends of sales guidance to forecast how much revenue is represented relative to Jakafi's current sales. Jakafi's sales in 2025 are 70.5% of the midpoint of $4.275B in sales, or $3.01B. Thus, if the lower CAGR is applicable 69% of the patent cliff will be offset in 2028. If the higher growth prevails, 82% of Jakafi sales will be offset by 2028.

Ex-Jakafi Scenario* 2030 Sales CAGR 2028 Projected Revenue Jakafi Sales Offset
Low Growth Case $3.0B 19.0% $2.12B 69%
High Growth Case $4.0B 26.0% $2.52B 82%

*Author's Estimates of Ex-Jakafi Sales by 2028.

The conclusion for investors is that the growth portfolio will largely offset the Jakafi patent cliff in a worst case scenario. While Jakafi is a small molecule and sales fall rapidly following generic competition, the speed of the decline is not 100%.

Two examples of small molecule drugs that saw patent cliffs following generic competition are Gleevec and Revlimid. Both drugs saw an approximate ~50% decline in revenue in the first year, stabilizing at ~10% of original sales thereafter. If Jakafi sales remain static until 2028, fall 50% in 2028 and then fall 80% in 2029 (10% of peak sales), the table below predicts revenues by year for the lower growth projection.

Revenue by Year* Jakafi Sales (2% growth; billions) Ex-Jakafi Sales (19% CAGR; in billions) Total Revenue (billions) YoY % Growth
2025 $3.01 $1.26 $4.27 --
2026 $3.07 $1.50 $4.57 +7.0%
2027 $3.13 $1.78 $4.91 +7.4%
2028 (Peak) $3.19 $2.12 $5.31 +8.2%
2029 (Cliff) $1.60 (-50%) $2.52 $4.12 -22.4%
2030 (Bottom) $0.32 (-80%) $3.00 $3.32 -19.4%
2031 (Recovery) $0.32 $3.57 $3.89 +17.2%

*Author's Estimates of Incyte Total Sales through 2030; Royalties Not Included.

The table above is instructive as a worst case scenario, assuming that the Ex-Jakafi portfolio can grow in line with the lower forecast growth rate. There are several assumptions that may prove to be too pessimistic:

  • While the launch of Jakafi XR has been delayed to mid-2026, expect Incyte following commercialization to protect sale from the cliff by transitioning as much as possible to the extended formulation.
  • The above table does not include any new products launched by Incyte between now and 2028.

Thus, in a lower growth scenario excluding Jakafi XR mitigation, there is an approximately $1.38B hole in 2028. However, this hole could be offset if 44% of Jakafi sales are moved to XR. There is historical precedent that this could be the case:

  • In 2002, Adderall XR was released by Shire. When generics arrived Shire was able to protect 60% of its sales with the extended formulation drug.
  • In 2007, Ambien's patent expired. However, Sanofi (SNY) pivoted in 2005 with an extended release version, ultimately protecting 60% of sales when the patent expired.

While, there are many less successful examples, it is reasonable to believe that a significant portion of the patent cliff can be buffered by Jakafi XR. The table below forecasts what our cliff scenario will look like if 50% of Jakafi sales are protected by moving to an extended release version.

Revenue by Year* Jakafi Sales (2% growth; billions) Jakafi XR Sales (billions) Ex-Jakafi Sales (19% CAGR; in billions) Total Revenue (billions) YoY % Growth
2025 $3.01 -- $1.26 $4.27 --
2026 $2.54 $0.53 $1.50 $4.57 +7.0%
2027 $2.07 $1.06 $1.78 $4.91 +7.4%
2028 (Peak) $1.60 $1.60 $2.12 $5.32 +8.4%
2029 (Cliff) $0.80 (-50%) $1.63 $2.52 $4.95 -7.0%
2030 (Bottom) $0.16 (-80%) $1.66 $3.00 $4.82 -2.6%
2031 (Recovery) $0.16 $1.70 $3.57 $5.43 +12.7%

*Author's Estimates of Incyte Total Sales through 2030; Royalties Not Included.

Thus, if the strategy of Jakafi XR works as well as several examples in the past, the patent cliff could become more of a patent slope. Moving on, we should consider the potential products Incyte may launch between now and 2028.

Incyte: Analyzing the Pipeline

Incyte's strategy for overcoming the patent cliff is to develop new products and the company outlined seven anticipated launches to accomplish this by 2031. In total, these products represent a $10B+ peak sales opportunity. To be clear, products launching from 2027-2031 will not have time to reach peak sales, there is not a realistic scenario where Incyte will achieve this level of sales growth by 2028. Further, some of these catalysts (e.g. Niktimvo) are label expansions that are included in Ex-Jakafi sales growth already.

However, there remain six potential commercialization catalysts between now and 2031 that could help to fill the patent cliff. Five out of six are currently in Phase 3. The Phase 3 catalysts include:

  • mutCALR: In June 2025, Phase 1 data supported advancing INCA033989 to Phase 3 studies. This targeted monoclonal antibody is set to begin Phase 3 studies by mid-2026. This is a targeted therapy for mutCALR patients with essential thrombocythemia.
  • TGFBR2xPD1: INCA33890 is a bispecific antibody engineered to avoid toxicity known to the TGFB pathway blockade. It specifically blocks cells co-expressing TGFB and PD-1. The Phase 3 trial is already underway.
  • KRAS-G12D: Perhaps the most exciting, INCB161734 has shown strong results in Phase 1 with a 37% ORR as monotherapy in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma (PDAC - a form of pancreatic cancer). It is an orally bioavailable small molecule and a pivotal Phase 3 study is planned to begin in 1H 2026. This is an extremely high unmet need (no targeted treatment options) and 40% of PDAC patients have the g12d mutation. Therefore, the drug has blockbuster potential, if approved.
  • CDK2i: INCB123667 is a novel, potent and selective oral small molecule inhibitor of CDK2. Following positive Phase 1 results, a pivotal Phase 3 is planned to begin in imminently.
  • Povorcitinib: This is an oral small molecule selective JAK1 inhibitor. This is essentially a "next-gen" Jakafi, binding only JAK1, rather than JAK1/3. This may present advantages in safety or efficacy, further derisking the patent cliff, if approved. Phase 3 trials are already ongoing.

While it is unknown if these drugs will be approved, they are all on track to deliver results by the time the patent cliff arrives.

Incyte: Analyst Forecasts - Still Too Bearish

Following the rebound in sales this year, analysts have become more optimistic in their assumptions. The strong growth of the past year will present difficult comparisons, thus do not be surprised if there is a period of consolidation after last year's surge.

The consensus analyst estimate is 11.2% sales growth in 2026. This is less than the 20% YoY growth rate in Q3 2025, but requires continued execution in the ex-Jakafi portfolio and may require some growth in Jakafi as well. I am not expecting a repeat of Q3 where YoY growth beats expectations by nearly 10%. However, if the mitigation strategies outlined above prove fruitful, then the -28.5% sales decline in 2029 will not be as dour as the market expects. It is even possible that the pipeline catalysts above could power Incyte past the patent cliff without losing much revenue in 2029.

If Incyte matches sales expectations in the coming year, then the stock trades for only 13.7x 2026 EPS estimates. Thus, the business is still priced for terminal decline. As investors and analysts realize that 2028/2029 will not be as dour as predicted, significant appreciation is still possible. My 12-month price target for the stock is 17x 2026 EPS of $7.70/share, or $130.9/share. If revenue growth continues, this would represent a PEG ratio of approximately 1.0x.

There is clearly little optimism priced into Incyte's stock. While management is projecting potentially $10B+ in new product potential in the coming years, the analyst consensus still expects inflationary growth from $4.98B to $5.69B in nine years (a 1.5% CAGR). For long-term investors Incyte's pipeline appears much brighter than the market is pricing in.

Conclusion

When I last covered Incyte in February 2025, the contrarian case was build on hope that the high number of commercialization catalysts could diversify the company away from Jakafi before the clock ran out in 2028. However, less than one year later, Incyte has outperformed my optimistic expectations.

Incyte has successfully executed a complex transformation, while returning Jakafi to growth in 2025. This led the stock to massively outperform Wall Street's expectations. Now the ex-Jakafi business is not just growing, it is thriving, up 45% YoY and scheduled to reach $3B-$4B by the end of this decade.

While year-over-year comparisons are more competitive than one year ago, I still believe that over the long-term analysts are too cautious due to the 2028 Jakafi patent cliff. They still attribute no growth to the pipeline even as it is poised to deliver many Phase 3 results. Further, Jakafi XR may dampen the patent cliff to a shallow hill by itself.

Incyte has massive potential with myriad exciting prospects in its pipeline, setting the stage for long-term growth. For growth oriented investors willing to see long-term potential past the 2028 patent cliff Incyte presents an attractive blend of risk and reward.

I reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Nvidia: Risks Continue To Loom - A Unique Value Trap For New Investors?
data.description:
In an article I wrote about Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG) last month, I outlined my thesis for these two hyperscalers. The gist of it was that they represent a threat to Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), not with a head-on trajectory, but as part of an evolution toward smaller language models overtaking large language models, or LLMs. I think that is more representative of an AI economy - the LLMs will be reserved for extremely intensive compute, while the bulk of localized AI workloads will either be processed locally, at the edge, but also in data centers running these smaller models for specific use cases.

Today, I want to look at this from Nvidia's viewpoint, and the perspective of risk.

Where is the Risk Really Coming From?

To set the stage, despite my Strong Sell stance on NVDA, which I am keeping, by the way, I absolutely love the business and the way it is run. Anyone who has read my three earlier articles on the CEO Jensen Huang-led juggernaut of data center compute will know this.

The issues I raise have nothing to do with the direction or momentum of the business but the direction and momentum of the stock, and the risk angle plays a massive role in that narrative.

There is no lack of evidence to support this. For instance, over the last twelve quarters, NVDA has maintained a pristine earnings beat record. Indeed, in the last two earnings reports, NVDA reported beats on both lines, with Q3 2026 growth rates of 60-percent-plus at the top and bottom. And yet, in those two quarters, the stock has barely gained 3 percent against a market growth of 7.4 percent.

Analogously speaking, I do not intend to show you a small piece of the elephant and expect you to buy the entire animal (in this case, my Strong Sell thesis) based on this one graph. Two graphs, perhaps, but the support for that thesis goes far beyond what I can show you in terms of price action right now.

The real legs of my elephant, if you will, are the irrefutable risks facing NVDA the stock, not necessarily Nvidia the company. One might make the mistake of assuming that fundamentals always catch up to valuations, but there is sufficient data to suggest that in this case, valuations may be trending down toward fundamental performance, making the risk look far less than it really is.

Let me demonstrate this.

The four charts above collectively inform us that the stock's price appreciation over the past three years has driven the sales multiple up by nearly two-thirds, but earnings multiple expansion is unable to keep up. Quarter after quarter of beating Street estimates on revenue growth has pushed the price up 1000 percent (split-adjusted), and because earnings growth just cannot keep up with this kind of price appreciation, NVDA's profitability multiples are suffering.

One might look at this and say, wait a minute, is it not a good thing that the stock is getting increasingly cheaper on a profitability basis? Well, it would seem that way because earnings growth itself remains strong. So, what is the issue here?

The problem, as I see it, is that this tends to act as a value trap. When you look at NVDA's forward earnings multiple of 39 and see that the sector median itself stands at 25, it does not look overpriced. However, put it into context along with sales multiples expanding, and one sees a divergence - not only between the share price increase and sales growth, but even more so between share price appreciation and earnings growth.

To put it simply, earnings are not growing fast enough to justify the rate of share price growth, and at the end of the day, earnings are what convert to cash flows, and when cash flow growth cannot keep up with share price increases, the price to cash flow multiple is going to get compressed over time.

The trap is that new investors might see this and be attracted to the declining multiples, but let us not forget that this is also where the downward pressure on the stock is coming from. At sky-high valuations, these growth rates cannot afford to falter.

In my last article, I spoke at length about how the news cycle was largely in control of the NVDA stock price narrative. Combine this with the fact that share price is down considerably since the 52-week high of $212 against an as-of-writing price of $187, and this despite its pristine consensus-beating record over that period, and one hopefully begins to see why my rating is still a Strong Sell.

Additional Context Around My Continued Strong Sell Thesis

Make no mistake, there are plenty of ways to make money off NVDA without owning the stock outright. Selling naked calls might look like they would work in such a situation, but this removes the cap on potential loss if the stock price spikes, as it often does due to the volatile nature of the stock.

A more capital-efficient option might be a bull call spread, where you end up with a net debt position on the premium but have a clearly defined upside to the higher strike of your sold calls. There are far riskier - and possibly more profitable - strategies, but as a risk-averse investor, this is the one I would recommend. One has a predefined upside as well as a predefined cost.

The advantage with a bull call spread right now is that one enjoys a volatility skew benefit that reduces the resulting net debit on a call spread.

OTM calls at higher strikes have higher implied volatility, pushing up their premiums.

As a hypothetical example only, if you bought Feb 6 contracts at $9.15 ($915 per contract) and a strike of $185 (slightly in the money) and sold OTM calls at at a strike of $200 for $2.54 ($254 per contract), your risk is already set at $661 per contract. The breakeven for this would be the difference between the strikes plus the cost of the spread, so $185 + $6.61, or $191.61. This means NVDA only needs to move up about 2.5 percent from the current price of $187 for you to recover your cost. The upside cap from there is the spread less the cost, so $15 - $6.61, or $839 per contract.

There are tons of other strategies to make money from options contracts, and if you are not well-versed with options, there are several leveraged ETFs that do the heavy lifting for you and take on the risk of direct ownership, and these have delivered stellar returns over the past two to three years.

The reason I outline these strategies is not solely for the benefit of those who do not want to own NVDA, but also investors whose portfolios are heavily overweight NVDA. These strategies are preemptively mitigatory, for the most part.

It is vital to reiterate that my Strong Sell does not imply an impending NVDA price crash. I do not see that happening because the fundamentals do not warrant it despite the high valuation multiples and the quirks that we looked at.

What I do see is an inadequate return to justify the humongous valuation risk that would accompany any sizeable NVDA holding at this point. The stock is very susceptible to the news cycle, as I have stated before, and buying pressure has been unable to push the stock past $200 again since it hit those peaks in November 2025, right after the Q3 2026 beats.

In past articles, I have spoken at length about the various risks faced by the stock, if not the company and its ongoing performance. I hope this article helps further that case, because all of the risks I outlined are still very present and clear. I am confident that I will continue to face criticism for this sustained viewpoint, but it is what it is - my opinion, and nothing more.

data.title: T-Mobile Is Starting To Get Interesting
data.description:
T-Mobile US (NASDAQ: TMUS) is an American wireless network operator worth almost $230 billion. The company underperformed the market by almost 25% in 2025, after we recommended selling, arguing that it was overvalued. Going forward, with its current valuation, we see T-Mobile US as an increasingly interesting investment.

T-Mobile Performance

T-Mobile has continued to maintain strong performance, as the company has continued to gather additional customers.

T-Mobile managed to add 2.4 million net customers within the quarter. The company finished the quarter with just under 140 million customers, a double-digit YoY improvement in customer count, and continued QoQ performance. The company's Broadband business remains minimal, with less than 9 million customers, but that's not surprising to us.

We view the true potential of 5G internet as an add-on opportunity, but relatively minimal versus the scale of cellular customers. Fundamentally 5G internet isn't as cheap and reliable as fiber internet and not as available remotely as satellite internet. However, a few million customers is still a nice add-on.

T-Mobile maintains a focus on reliable and high-quality 5G coverage. The company has a strong band of spectrum, partially supported by the Sprint acquisition. T-Mobile realized early on in the 5G race that spectrum and speeds were key to success, something that made AT&T and Verizon spend $10s of billions on expensive spectrum to catch up.

That's key to T-Mobile's future success and growth.

T-Mobile Financial Picture

However, at the end of the day T-Mobile's financial picture and cash flow is key to future returns.

T-Mobile has managed to consistently maintain strong adjusted FCF. The company's quarterly FCF declined YoY but YTD for the first 3Q the company managed to increase its FCF by almost 7%. The 6.7% adjusted free cash flow growth is in line with 6.8% total service revenue growth and 7.5% total revenue growth, along with 6.4% adjusted EBITDA growth.

It's also impressive that the company managed to do this, while maintaining capital expenditures, with 12.9% in capex growth.

Annualized, the company's adjusted FCF is $18.4 billion, or an almost 9% adjusted FCF yield. That's incredibly strong adjusted FCF and enough for the company to continue driving substantial shareholder returns.

T-Mobile Guidance

T-Mobile's guidance is for incredibly strong continued performance.

The company is expected $17.9 billion adjusted FCF for the year, tightening up its guidance at the upper end. Capex is expected to remain strong with this FCF with $10 billion invested in the company's network. This is based on $600 million in net cash provided by operating activities in the midpoint.

With continued customer growth, this guidance shows T-Mobile's impressive portfolio.

T-Mobile Shareholder Returns

This strong cash flow enables T-Mobile to continue providing lofty shareholder returns.

The company spent $3.5 billion on shareholder returns in the most recent quarter, annualized at $14 billion. That's more than 70% of the company's adjusted FCF. The company maintains a respectable 2.2% dividend yield, though we'd like to see the company increase that similar to its peers dividend yield.

T-Mobile has managed to substantially reduce its outstanding shares and we'd like to see it continuing to do so. The company has repurchased an impressive almost $35 billion of stock and as long as it can continue doing so, that positions well to provide long-term shareholder returns. As we saw above, the company has high single-digit FCF even with continued invest in growth.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the competition that T-Mobile faces. AT&T and Verizon have both made massive spectrum purchases to bring their 5G portfolio up to par. As they improve their spectrum, we're concerned that they'll be able to find new customers, which could hurt T-Mobile's pricing and shareholder returns.

Conclusion

T-Mobile has, supported by its Sprint acquisition has built up an incredibly impressive 5G portfolio. The company has added millions of customers and it plans to continue adding millions more customers. That has enabled the company to generate high single-digit FCF which it has used to generate strong repurchases and dividends.

Going forward, we expect T-Mobile to continue increasing its FCF at the mid-single-digits as it has been. That enables T-Mobile to not only continue its lofty shareholder returns but to continue increasing those returns making it a valuable long-term investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Pfizer: Rebound Has Just Started
data.description:
Pfizer (PFE) is gaining share price momentum as the stock notably outperformed the S&P 500 Index since my previous bullish coverage. Improving momentum is justified by positive developments around Pfizer's fundamentals, which are likely to result in long-term financial stability. As a result, I believe that PFE's hefty 6.7% forward dividend yield is rock-solid. This is a crucial quality in today's uncertain environment which might force investors rotate from growth stocks to quality dividend plays like PFE. Moreover, Pfizer looks very attractively valued, which also makes it a compelling investment opportunity. Therefore, I continue buying PFE with reiterated strong buy rating.

Reasons to continue buying PFE

One of the main reasons why investors were cautious about PFE over the last few years is the instability of its earnings surprise record, particularly from the revenue standpoint. However, this situation is improving as PFE delivered second dual beat in a row in Q3 2025. It is a solid improvement, and every next dual earnings beat will likely continue increasing investors' confidence in PFE. Moreover, the company demonstrates improving operating margins despite revenue growth headwinds. It means that the management is delivering on their promises to keep costs under control and to achieve a few billion in structural cost savings. For a value stock like PFE, the cost side is sometimes even more important as investors are aware that it is difficult for such a mature company to drive aggressive revenue growth. Therefore, Pfizer's management is doing a great job in creating more value for shareholders. Meanwhile, I am glad that the management is not cutting the R&D budget significantly, as it is vital for creating long-term growth catalysts. Pfizer's current annualized R&D spending of approximately $10+ billion indicates that the management is not sacrificing innovation.

As a result of aggressive investments in innovation, Pfizer currently has more than 100 candidates in its pipeline, out of which one-third is either under registration or Phase 3 trials. Even if only 20% of these potential assets get final approvals, it means approximately 6-7 new innovative products for Pfizer. During the 44th Annual JPMorgan Healthcare Conference, Pfizer's management has revealed some of the most promising near-term catalysts. I believe that the most significant ones for 2026 are the Metsera obesity data (GLP-1) and SV (sigvotatug vedotin) lung cancer readout. The main reason why I spotlight these two prospective assets is that both of them will mean expansion into large and rapidly growing markets.

Over the last few years, Eli Lilly (LLY) has become the world's by far largest pharmaceutical company with almost a trillion-dollar market cap thanks to its rapidly growing GLP-1 business. It is not only because of LLY's excellent execution, but also because this domain of the global pharmaceutical market is growing very aggressively. Analysts from UBS expect the GLP-1 market to be worth approximately $130 billion by 2029, which means that this industry is going to be large enough not only for the Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk (NVO) oligopoly but for other companies to capitalize on this structural tailwind as well. Moreover, Metsera obesity offerings will have differentiation by offering monthly dosing, which looks more convenient than the current weekly dosing standard proposed by LLY and NVO.

And the lung cancer treatment market is also rapidly growing and is poised to become much larger over the long-term. The main reason is the fact that lung cancer is the most common cancer worldwide with approximately 2 million new cases every year. Moreover, lung cancer also accounts for the highest mortality rates among both men and women. Therefore, I think that the double-digit long-term growth rate forecasted for the lung cancer therapeutics market is not an overly aggressive expectation. This area of medicine needs more novel approaches to treat this deadly disease, and that is the reason why Pfizer's management believes that SV has potential to become a blockbuster drug.

I think that Pfizer building its foothold in these two rapidly growing markets has potential to become robust long-term growth catalysts for the company, taking into account strong pipeline in these areas. Therefore, I think that Pfizer's extremely cheap valuation is likely to be temporary.

I am confident that PFE is undervalued because it is not only significantly cheaper than its historical multiples, but is also cheaper compared to the sector. And these are not just small discrepancies but large gaps between PFE's valuation ratios and the sector median. Moreover, Wall Street analysts suggest that PFE is currently approximately 11% undervalued. While a low double-digit upside might not be attractive enough for some investors, it is vital to keep in mind that Wall Street's consensus price depends on EPS growth projections. And these projections might see substantial positive revisions after positive developments around PFE's pipeline.

Finally, PFE investors are highly likely to benefit from the cooling sentiment around growth stocks due to increasing "AI bubble" related concerns. I think that share price performance of Nvidia (NVDA) is a great barometer to determine the market's sentiment around AI revolution. Nvidia released its latest earnings on November 19, which was a really strong release with a confident outlook shared by Jensen Huang. However, Nvidia's share price grew by 0.3% since November 19, notably underperforming compared to the broad market. Such dynamic might indicate rapidly deteriorating excitement around AI-exposed stocks, which could lead to rotation from growth to value. As a deeply undervalued dividend champion with a few strong catalysts on the horizon, PFE might be a great safe harbor for investors seeking rotation.

Reasons to avoid PFE

Despite Pfizer's stock gaining momentum since my previous bullish article, its share price performance over the longer-term might spook some investors. The stock is down by 1.3% over the last twelve months, while both the S&P 500 Index and the healthcare sector demonstrated growth. Some investors might decide not to invest after looking at this underperformance. Moreover, even almost a 7% forward dividend yield might not be enough to persuade investors given Pfizer's 30% share price decline over the last five years.

While Pfizer's profitability has been improving over the last few quarters thanks to the management's cost saving initiatives, there is still notable room for improvement. It is evident from the fact that Pfizer's current ROIC is approximately five percentage points lower than the average value for the last decade. This is a remarkable potential drawback because I already mentioned that Pfizer invests about $10 billion per year in R&D, meaning that even a few percentage points underperformance in terms of ROIC means large amounts dollar-wise.

Finally, the current geopolitical environment is becoming increasingly uncertain, which is also a risk for Pfizer. The company operates internationally and sources raw materials for production also internationally. Therefore, potential escalation in trade wars or adverse changes in foreign exchange rates might substantially affect Pfizer's earnings.

The verdict

PFE remains a strong buy, in my opinion. The management is currently focused on driving EPS growth via cost efficiency, and positive developments around the pipeline are expected in 2026 as well. Every new FDA approval has the potential to boost the company's long-term EPS growth potential.

data.title: Electrons, Intelligence, And The Shape Of The Next Economy: 2025 Year-End Macro Commentary
data.description:
The defining question of 2025 wasn’t whether artificial intelligence would transform the economy. It was whether intelligence alone would be enough.

Silicon Valley spent the year betting everything on the proposition that whoever builds the most capable AI models wins the future. China made a different wager: that intelligence becomes a commodity, and strategic value accrues to whoever can power it, deploy it, and embed it in physical systems at scale. One side is building “God in a box.” The other is building the infrastructure to make gods useful.

We think China has the better end of this bet. Not because American AI labs aren’t producing remarkable capabilities—they are (Anthropic’s Opus 4.5 and Gemini Pro 3 Thinking are in fact extremely competent and were essential partners in synthesizing my input sources and in developing this narrative). But because the United States is making a leveraged wager on a single form of technology while systematically ceding much of the physical capacity to manufacture, deploy, and benefit from whatever that technology produces. This is not a strategy. It is a hope that intelligence can overcome physics, logistics, and industrial ecosystems. History suggests otherwise.

So what follows is what we learned in 2025 about the forces shaping the next decade of economic competition—and what it means for capital allocation in an era defined by the collision of technological acceleration and planetary constraints. In other words, the view from Next Economics.

The Electric Stack: What Actually Matters

The most important piece of economic analysis published in 2025 was Packy McCormick’s 40,000-word history of what he calls “the Electric Stack“—the four layers of technology that convert electricity into motion and work: lithium-ion batteries, neodymium magnets and electric motors, power electronics (the semiconductors that switch currents thousands of times per second), and embedded compute (the microcontrollers that orchestrate everything in real-time).

These four technologies share something remarkable: a composite cost curve showing 99% decline since 1990. Batteries, magnets, power electronics, and embedded compute have each fallen at roughly 12-15% per year for three decades. This is the foundation on which electric vehicles, drones, robots, grid storage, and, increasingly, the physical infrastructure of AI itself are built. Master this stack, and you can build almost anything the modern economy demands. Cede it, and you’re probably buying the future from someone else.

This is the Jevons Paradox of the Next Economy: as these four technologies become orders of magnitude more efficient to manufacture and deploy, we do not use less of them. Instead, we find thousands of new ways to embed them into the very fabric of existence. Efficiency, in this context, is not a path to conservation—it is the catalyst for a total physical takeover.

China has mastered it. The United States is ceding it.

The numbers are stark. China controls approximately 75% of global lithium-ion battery production (CATL alone commands 38% market share). It manufactures 90% of the world’s neodymium magnets. It dominates solar panels, wind turbines, grid equipment, and the electric vehicles and drones built on these components. In 2024, China added 429 gigawatts of new power capacity—more than a third of the entire US grid (wind and solar power accounted for the vast majority of China’s new capacity, totaling 356.5 GW, about 83% of the total increase). America added 51 gigawatts.

How did this happen? The same pattern repeats across every layer of the stack: American or European scientists invent the core technology, fail to commercialize it at scale, and eventually watch as patient Chinese industrial policy, vertical integration, and relentless manufacturing iteration capture the value.

Lithium-ion batteries were invented by an American (John Goodenough), refined by a Japanese chemist (Akira Yoshino), commercialized by Sony, and then absorbed into the Chinese manufacturing ecosystem when A123 Systems went bankrupt in 2012 and was sold to China’s Wanxiang for $257 million—a fire sale from a $2.4 billion valuation. Today, BYD and CATL, still leveraging but now having far exceeded A123’s IP, dominate global production. The IEA projects marginal reduction in Chinese market share by 2030.

Neodymium magnets were simultaneously discovered by researchers in Japan and at General Motors in 1983. GM subsequently sold its Magnequench division to a consortium connected to Deng Xiaoping’s sons-in-law for $70 million. Today, China controls 90% of global neodymium magnet production and 85-90% of rare earth processing.

The IGBT—the power semiconductor that made modern motor control possible—was invented at General Electric by B. Jayant Baliga. Jack Welch sold GE’s semiconductor division in 1988. Japanese firms commercialized the technology. Now China’s CRRC (which bought the UK’s Dynex in 2008) and BYD are racing to the frontier of silicon carbide power electronics (see The Electric Slide by McCormick, above).

This is not a story of Chinese theft. It is a story of American financialization—of corporations that consistently chose short-term returns over long-term capability, that sold strategic assets for quarterly earnings, that outsourced manufacturing because labor and energy were cheaper elsewhere without understanding that the learning happens on the factory floor.

As Dan Wang put it in his annual letter: “American scientists may be world leaders in dreaming up new ideas. But American manufacturers have been poor at building industries around these ideas. Bell Labs invented the first solar cell in 1957; today, the lab no longer exists while the solar industry moved to Germany and then to China.”

The Electrostate and the Petrostate

While the 20th century was won by those who controlled the flow of oil, the 21st will be won by those who control the intelligence of the grid

Eurasia Group’s 2026 risk report crystallizes the divergence in a single phrase: “Washington is asking the world to buy 20th-century energy while Beijing offers 21st-century infrastructure.”

In 2010, China was arguably the most fossil fuel-dependent major economy on Earth. Today, it is the first “electrostate”—the world’s largest consumer and producer of clean energy, with most of its growth in power capacity coming from renewables. The United States, meanwhile, has cemented its status as the world’s largest petrostate, pumping 13.5 million barrels of oil per day, and having surpassing Saudi Arabia in 2018.

This matters for more than climate. It matters for economic competition.

China’s value proposition to emerging markets is compelling: solar panels and wind turbines that don’t rely on volatile commodity imports, battery storage systems, grid equipment, electric vehicles—all getting cheaper and more capable by the day. Chinese exports of renewable technologies have now surpassed US fossil fuel exports. In the global vehicle market of 85 million units annually, 20 million are now EVs. China provides 10% of that market through exports alone, and Chinese EVs hold a 75% share of EV sales in emerging markets.

The US response has been to retreat further into its fossil fuel position. The “big beautiful bill” phases out tax credits for utility-scale solar and wind while adding restrictions that make battery credits more difficult to claim. This isn’t energy strategy. It’s culture war.

The result, as Eurasia Group frames it, is a “triple bind” for the United States: higher energy costs and slower buildouts domestically; ceding influence in the fastest-growing economies to China internationally; and wagering strategically that intelligence alone wins when the physical stack that powers and deploys intelligence may be equally or more important.

What’s Actually Working in the Energy Transition

It’s worth stepping back from the geopolitical competition to assess what the energy transition has accomplished.

Gregor Macdonald, one of the most rigorous energy analysts writing today, anchors his analysis to 2010—the year utility-scale wind and solar started landing with force, EV adoption became visible as a sustainable trend, and national policy strategies emerged with clarity.

Since then, combined wind and solar has grown from 1.76% to 17% of global power generation. That’s an enormous accomplishment in a short time span, avoiding a very large tranche of emissions growth. Global sales of internal combustion engine vehicles peaked in 2017. China is now at nearly 50% EV market share for passenger and commercial vehicles combined. Global gasoline consumption is flatlining.

The technologies that are working share common characteristics: they’re already on proven cost curves, they’re manufactured at scale, and they don’t require new infrastructure paradigms. Solar panels, wind turbines, batteries, EVs, heat pumps—these are established technologies, no longer experimental.

What’s not working? The hard-to-abate sectors: steel, cement, air travel, industrial heat. Fifteen years into the transition, we’re still at the experimental, proof-of-concept stage for green steel, hydrogen in aviation, and decarbonized industrial processes. Nothing is scaling. Why? Price. The economics don’t yet work.

And hydrogen—despite years of hype about its versatility—remains largely on the drawing board while established tech like heat pumps actually delivers. As Macdonald notes, heat pumps have taken a far bigger stride in lowering natural gas consumption in Europe than any hydrogen application.

This distinction matters for capital allocation. The technologies riding proven cost curves will continue to gain share. The technologies that require new infrastructure and haven’t yet demonstrated economic viability remain speculative, which isn’t to say entirely without promise. Investors should know the difference.

AI in Context: Powerful but Not Sufficient

None of this diminishes the significance of artificial intelligence. The capabilities emerging from frontier labs are real and consequential. Models now reason through complex problems, show their work, and are embedded in coding, research, and knowledge workflows. Ivan Zhao of Notion reports that co-founders have gone from “10x programmers” to “30-40x” by orchestrating multiple AI agents simultaneously. Per METR, AI can now reliably complete tasks that take humans about an hour; extrapolating the current doubling rate, eight-hour autonomous workstreams become plausible by late 2026.

But AI operates under constraints that its most enthusiastic proponents tend to ignore.

To understand why physical capacity is the final bottleneck, one must look at where AI is currently hitting its hardest wall: the complex, messy world of biology. Biology provides the clearest proof that ground truth cannot be ‘hallucinated’—it must be discovered. Bo Wang, a leading researcher in AI and biology, offered a sharp corrective in his year-end reflections: “Biology is not ‘just another modality.’ It’s causal, hierarchical, stochastic, and incomplete in ways that language and vision are not. Scaling mostly gives you sharper correlations without causal structure.” Real progress in biological AI requires perturbation and experimentation—physical intervention in the real world, not just better models.

This pattern extends beyond biology. In manufacturing, you have to actually make things to learn how to make them better. In energy, electrons still have to flow through physical infrastructure. AI accelerates cognition, but it doesn’t escape the domains where ground truth requires physical intervention.

The rate of acceleration varies enormously by domain. Code and language offer fast feedback loops and clean ground truth—hence rapid progress. Biology, healthcare, energy, and manufacturing involve slower feedback, messy ground truth, and capital-intensive physical systems. Progress in these domains will be constrained by the pace of physical experimentation and buildout, regardless of how capable the models become.

The true value of AI in the Next Economy isn’t in generating text, but in System Orchestration. We are moving from a ‘Passive Stack’ to an ‘Active Stack.’ An AI that can manage a city-scale virtual power plant, balancing millions of bidirectional EV charges with fluctuating wind output, is worth more than a ‘God’ that can write a legal brief. This is the ‘Orchestration Layer’—the software that makes the physical stack intelligent. In the Next Economy, the ultimate ‘moat’ is not found in the elegance of an algorithm, but in the intelligence of the physical system that converts an electron into work.

Ultimately, while Silicon Valley builds a God to answer questions, Beijing is building the nervous system for a world that no longer needs to ask permission from the Petrostate.

This is the core error in the “AI solves everything” thesis. AI is a powerful general-purpose accelerant, but it doesn’t transcend the physical systems in which it must be embedded to create value. And those physical systems—the Electric Stack, the manufacturing capacity, the energy infrastructure—are increasingly controlled by China.

The Bet America Is Making

Dan Wang frames the strategic choice clearly: “The US economy is increasingly a highly leveraged bet on deep learning.”

The bet is that whoever achieves artificial general intelligence first gains a “decisive strategic advantage”—a technology sufficient to achieve comprehensive dominance across economic, military, and geopolitical dimensions. Build the superintelligence, and everything else follows.

Wang is skeptical, and so are we.

China is not years behind on AI. DeepSeek and Qwen are doggedly in pursuit, sometimes closer to US frontier models, sometimes further. By virtue of being open-source (or open-weight), Chinese models have found receptive customers overseas—sometimes including American tech companies. If US labs achieve superintelligence, Chinese labs are probably positioned to follow closely. Unless the advantage is decisive immediately, the US won’t have a monopoly on this technology. In reality, the January 2026 breakthroughs from DeepSeek have effectively ended the era of ‘compute-as-a-moat,’ proving that world-class intelligence can be distilled for a fraction of the cost—leaving physical infrastructure as the only remaining barrier to entry.

More fundamentally, capability without deployment is insufficient. Silicon Valley hasn’t demonstrated joined-up thinking for diffusing AI throughout society, which would require extensive regulatory and legal reform, workforce transition, and infrastructure investment. “How else will AI be able to fold doctors and lawyers into its tender mercies?” Wang asks. Beijing, by contrast, has set targets for deploying AI across society and is embedding it into robots, manufacturing lines, and physical infrastructure.

The result: the US may be using AI to produce more PowerPoints and lawsuits while China, by virtue of being the preeminent global manufacturer, scales up production of electronics, drones, and military systems. Intelligence without action is insufficient. And China is betting that controlling the physical stack lets them commoditize intelligence and still win.

Next Economics: Who Captures Value from the Transition

The energy transition is happening. The question is who captures the value.

From a Next Economics perspective—an idealized post-scarcity economy operating within planetary boundaries—the goal isn’t just “more growth” or “winning against China” or “capturing value from AI.” The goal is prosperity that doesn’t undermine its own physical foundations.

Hannah Ritchie‘s framework is useful here. Three statements are almost always simultaneously true: the world is awful, the world is much better, and the world can be much better. High-income, low-energy countries don’t exist—development requires energy abundance. But when that energy comes from combustion, progress comes at environmental cost that eventually reverses the development gains.

Electrification can break this tradeoff. Low-carbon electricity generation plus broad electrification of the global energy system can deliver energy abundance without the combustion externalities. This is why the Electric Stack matters: it’s the physical infrastructure for decoupling development from environmental degradation.

The companies solving systemic risks—climate crisis, resource degradation, disease burdens, inequality—rather than perpetuating them are the companies building the Next Economy. The companies whose products and services are leading the way to a post-scarcity world are building the Next Economy. The investment opportunity isn’t in the incumbents defending legacy systems. It’s in the firms riding the cost curves that are already working: batteries, solar, wind, EVs, heat pumps, grid storage, power electronics.

But here’s the uncomfortable reality: most of those firms are currently Chinese. BYD, CATL, LONGi, Sungrow, Huawei’s solar inverters—the companies manufacturing the Electric Stack at scale are overwhelmingly based in China. American and European firms are attempting to rebuild domestic capacity, but they’re starting from far behind.

This creates a strategic dilemma. The technologies that serve planetary boundaries are increasingly manufactured by a geopolitical rival. Deploying them globally advances the energy transition while deepening dependence on Chinese supply chains. Restricting them slows the transition while fragmenting the global economy and leaves the West at an economic disadvantage (consider how much cheaper it is to manufacture cars and phones with zero marginal cost renewable electricity than with expensive fossil fuel electricity).

While China currently dominates the ‘first mine’—the extraction and refining of virgin lithium, cobalt, and rare earths—the Next Economy provides a second path: Molecular Re-manufacturing. If the Electric Stack is the hardware of the future, the molecules within that hardware are the new permanent capital. Unlike a gallon of gasoline, which is destroyed upon use, a kilogram of lithium is an asset that can be used, recovered, and re-deployed indefinitely. The West’s strategic ‘out’ isn’t just trying to out-dig China; it is in mastering the high-affinity chemistry required to recycle these materials at a lower cost than mining them.

We are seeing the early stages of this shift. Companies like Redwood Materials and Li-Cycle (and emerging European players) are betting that the ‘mine’ of 2030 will be the scrap pile and the spent battery pack. In a Next Economy framework, this is the ultimate expression of decoupling: achieving energy abundance without the need for continuous, destructive extraction. Controlling the Circular Stack—the technology to recover and re-upcycle the Electric Stack—is where the West can build a durable, localized, and geopolitically independent advantage. We are shifting from an era of Resource Extraction to an era of Resource Orchestration—where the winner is not who owns the most molecules, but who loops them most efficiently.

But until the Circular Stack scales beyond Redwood Materials, there is no clean resolution. The US bet on molecules while China bet on electrons. The consequences of that divergence are for now unavoidable.

And yet, even outside of the Circular Stack, China’s dominance isn’t absolute, and the cost curves that built Chinese leadership are available to others willing to ride them. In batteries, Korea’s LG Energy Solution and Samsung SDI remain credible competitors, and emerging American players like QuantumScape Corp. are pursuing higher energy-density chemistries. In power electronics, Wolfspeed leads globally in silicon carbide substrates (33.7% market share), even after a difficult restructuring—and the shift to SiC is early enough that leadership remains contestable. Vulcan Elements, backed by $65 million in Series A funding, is building decoupled neodymium magnet manufacturing in Durham, North Carolina, betting that better chemistry and modern manufacturing can undercut Chinese pricing. Impulse Labs is embedding batteries in induction stoves, partnering with appliance OEMs to capture volume in markets where “made in America” carries procurement advantages. Grid-scale battery storage, HVDC transmission, and nuclear are all poised to scale and aren’t exclusively Chinese domains. The pattern across these opportunities: companies riding proven cost curves in segments where Western manufacturing can still establish footholds, often where national security considerations create protected demand or where integration with local infrastructure provides advantages that offset Chinese cost leadership. These are the spaces we’re watching most closely.

What We’re Watching in 2026

Several dynamics bear close attention in the year ahead.

Fleet turnover will remain a binding constraint. Even cheap new technology can’t displace economically viable legacy infrastructure. The 2010 Toyota in someone’s garage still runs. The natural gas plant built in 2015 still produces electricity cheaper than the capital cost of a new solar installation. Displacement happens at the margin—new demand and retiring old capacity—not through wholesale replacement of functioning systems. This is why the transition takes decades, not years. See Macdonald, above.

AI budget scrutiny will intensify. The land-grab phase is ending. Tunguz predicts buying committees and boards will push back on AI spending for the first time, with small language models and open-source alternatives rising as companies seek 10x cost reductions. The commoditization that McCormick and Wang predict is already visible. DeepSeek’s breakthroughs illustrate the Jevons Paradox in real-time: by making frontier-level intelligence 90% cheaper to produce, they haven’t lowered the world’s ‘intelligence budget’—they’ve simply ensured that AI will be integrated into billions of devices that were previously too ‘dumb’ to be economical.

The deployment gap will become more visible. Capability and deployment are different things. The US leads in frontier model development but lags in the infrastructure to power and deploy AI at scale. China adds power capacity at 8x the US rate. This divergence will increasingly constrain American AI (and manufacturing) ambitions.

The infrastructure demands are staggering. Tomasz Tunguz estimates data center buildout will reach 3.5% of US GDP in 2026—a scale of investment comparable to the railroad expansion of the 19th century. But unlike the railroads, this buildout faces bottlenecks the market alone can’t solve: interconnection queues averaging over eight years, aging transmission infrastructure, and community opposition that has killed projects across multiple states. The fastest, cheapest path to new capacity—solar plus batteries, deployable in 18 months—is precisely what current US policy is hobbling.

Physical infrastructure investments will compound. The companies building batteries, power electronics, motors, and grid infrastructure are positioned to benefit regardless of which AI models ultimately dominate. Physical capacity is durable in ways that software leadership often isn’t.

Political dysfunction will impede coherent strategy. The US is in the midst of what Eurasia Group calls a “political revolution whose outcome will remain genuinely indeterminate for years.” Coherent industrial policy is difficult when institutions are in flux. This favors China’s patient, long-term approach over American improvisation.

Closing Thoughts

We called our investment approach “Next Economy Portfolio Theory” because we believe the economy that emerges from the current transition will operate on fundamentally different principles than the one that preceded it. The companies that thrive will be those solving systemic risks rather than creating them—building the infrastructure for prosperity and post-scarcity within planetary boundaries.

The transition is happening. Wind and solar have grown from under 2% to 17% of global power generation in fifteen years. Electric vehicles are approaching 25% of new car sales globally. The cost curves are working. The physics is on our side.

But the transition is also being won, in manufacturing terms, by China. The Electric Stack is largely Chinese. The deployment capacity is increasingly Chinese. The strategic patience is Chinese.

The United States can still compete. It has advantages in innovation, capital markets, and talent attraction that remain formidable. But competing requires recognizing that intelligence alone is insufficient—that physical capacity matters, that manufacturing is where learning happens, and that you can’t outsource your way to strategic advantage.

We’re not optimistic about American policymakers reaching this recognition anytime soon. The culture war over energy, the financialized short-termism of corporate governance, the political dysfunction that makes long-term strategy nearly impossible—these are not problems that resolve quickly.

But here’s what we’ve learned in eighteen years of managing capital toward the Next Economy: policy follows markets more often than it leads them. When solar becomes the cheapest electron, utilities and hyperscalers build solar—regardless of what politicians say about coal. When EVs outperform on cost and capability, consumers buy EVs—regardless of what culture warriors say about truck masculinity. The economics creates facts on the ground that policy eventually accommodates. Here’s my rough mental model that categorizes the 2026 landscape:

Category The “Old” Bet (Petrostate) The “Hype” Bet (Pure Software) The “Next” Bet (Integrated Stack)
Primary Asset Molecules (Oil/Gas) Weights/Biases (Models) Electrons + Hardware + Logic
Resource Model Linear (Extract/Discard) Virtual (Infinite Scalability) Circular (Molecular Loops)
Bottleneck Geopolitical Conflict Compute/Capital Interconnection & Physical Space
Value Capture Rent-seeking/Extraction Subscription (SaaS)/Advertising Efficiency Gains & System Resilience

This is why capital allocation matters. Every dollar directed toward companies building the Electric Stack, riding the cost curves, solving the systemic risks, greatly improving productivity—that’s a dollar accelerating the transition that policy alone cannot and never will deliver. It’s not charity. These companies are winning because their economics are winning. But it is a choice about what kind of economy we’re building, and who benefits from it.

We founded Green Alpha in 2007 on the conviction that the economy of the future would be built by companies solving problems rather than creating them. Eighteen years later, that conviction has only deepened. The Next Economy isn’t a hope or a projection. It’s under construction, right now, in battery factories and solar fields and grid infrastructure and the physical systems that both make intelligence and make intelligence useful.

The Next Economy is not a destination we are waiting for; it is a system we are currently auditing. Our mandate for 2026 is to move capital away from the friction of the Petrostate and toward the orchestration of the Electrostate. We aren’t betting on a cleaner world—we are betting on a smarter, safer, more resilient one. The physics demands it; the economics ensure it.

The question isn’t whether to participate. The question is whether we participate fast enough—whether capital flows to the solutions at the pace that the risks require. That’s the work. That’s what we’re here for.

data.title: Indra Sistemas: The Valuation Is Too High In 2026
data.description:
If you've been an investor in Indra Sistemas (ISMAY) during 2025, chances are it's time for me (and other analysts who did not) to congratulate you. After all, you invested where others did not and where the upside has been rewarding. The company has surged well over 100% this year, moving from a native price of about €16/share to over €57/share. That's a massive return for a company of this size and of these fundamentals. In this article, I will be providing my first coverage for the company, as well as a, from what I can tell by looking at SA valuation and target trends, a counterpoint to what coverage there is. Wall Street doesn't cover the company - Quant coverage is also non-existent, and what coverage does exist focuses on the upside in defense in Europe, and lauds the company as a player with more upside.

I am not saying that these points are invalid at all - what I am saying is that the valuation given to these growth metrics and forecasts, when considering company fundamentals and historicals, do not necessarily make the most sense.

Based on this, I'm happy to "throw my hat in the ring", as it were, and provide my own view on what this company should be worth. This, as indicated, will be my very first coverage of the company.

For US/NA-based investors, this company trades on the MSE, the Madrid stock exchange, under the native ticker IDR, and has, among others, the ADR ISMAY. As usual, my humble suggestion would be to focus on investing not in the ADR, but in the native, where possible, as many brokers now offer native access trading. I suggest this for reasons of liquidity as well as currency diversification.

That being said, let's get going here.

Indra Sistemas S.A - Finding upside in overvaluation

Many are the companies that have seen the way the wind is blowing in Europe for the past few years. Indra Sistemas is one of them. Not a pure-play defense player in any way in the sense that it manufactures munitions or anything of the sort, it's an IT-oriented service and hardware provider. If you look at the company description, it's a generalized overview of details consulting, project, integration, IT outsourcing, and Business processes, or BPO.

This could, of course, describe thousands of businesses in Europe. What makes Indra unique is its exposure to the defense sector, not just in Spain, but other countries.

It works with things like aviation technology, focusing on radar, electronic warfare, simulators, and UAV technology. However, as you might expect for an older company (because after all, it's been around for at least 30 years), it can't have survived that long with just military tech. It's less than €300M net income (which is high for 2024A) isn't enough to justify an upside if that was the case, based on its growth and revenue. Instead, it also has a civil segment, where the company is a major player in ATM technology.

Air traffic management, as the skies are getting more congested, is getting more and more important. And Indra offers a third of the world's ATM systems.

This is the civil upside. To say that I have been unaware of the company is wrong - to say that I didn't expect it to outperform, that on the other hand would be an accurate statement. Aside from the ATM segment, it's military appeal was at best "so-so" before Ukraine and the current geopolitical situation, and it's other segments, such as mobility was more exposed to competition, and it's Minsait, LATAM-focused segment was a bit too unstable for my taste. The fact that it's yield was also less than 1% even at the best of times, saw me consider other companies as far more attractive than Indra.

It's not that Indra is too local - it's operations are found in over 40 countries across the globe, but much of its upside and operations are too ethereal and too forward-looking and uncertain for me to have paid them much serious mind, until the military saw more appeal in 2025.

The company's goals and its earnings have seen me reconsider its overall upside here, however. While it won't be an easy road to get where the company's going, I'm prepared to grant it a bigger-than-previous role in European and potentially international defense roles, backed up by the aforementioned strong civil air traffic management segment. However, the EBIT split above should make it clear to you why I haven't given the company all that much focus before. Even though ATM has been a part of the company for some time, before the military growth, it was mostly a Minsait (which again, I consider volatile and somewhat uncertain) and a smaller ATM segment. Mobility is so small that it almost, at this point, fails to hold much relevance next to defense and Minsait segment results.

So the reason the company is getting more attractive here is the upside in military/defense.

I say this because Minsait has at best been a somewhat speculative "arm" of the company, with the company rumored to be looking to divest it (at least there have been offers from various PE funds) or parts of it, forming an uncertainty for investors. This uncertainty causes market fluctuations, which I given the lack of visibility for both defense and the company's non-ATM-focused segments, have hard a hard time putting a discount or a price on.

The one thing that makes Indra more interesting to me now, and which also differentiates it from your bread-and-butter IT companies in the military, is that it offers end-to-end integrated product solutions. It uses proprietary suites and is able to integrate hardware from third-party solutions as well. And this is also the operations that it's expanding, with things like new radar as well as being part of the FCAS, or Future Combat Air System, which is part of the most advanced current defense project in Europe.

So, Indra in 2026 is certainly more interesting than Indra from about 2-3 years ago.

But, this does not mean that the concentration risk or volatility is over.

As you can see above, the lion's share of revenue concentration and EBITDA breakdown comes from Spain and Minsait, respectively. Minsait, in my view, lacks the competitive advantage of some of the company's defense operations, and sees impact from other IT companies like Accenture (ACN) and CapGemini (CGEMY), both of which I cover here on Seeking Alpha - and those are just the bigger companies, with dozens of smaller ones vying for market space as well.

So whenever a company with that exposure starts trading up 100-300%, one of the first things I do to avoid a crash down is to see what sort of justification we have for that sort of upswing.

In this case, my short answer is some, but not as much as that.

Valuation for Indra Sistemas - Tricky, despite what the market may tell you

Often, a picture will tell you more than words. This is one of those times, even if I am going to be supplementing that picture with clear wording.

The conviction or assumption that a company can completely disentangle itself from its historical trends is one that I've found to be flawed in most companies I cover. What you see, therefore, is a company that over the past 20 years can best be characterized as "troubled", and has gone negative several times, only to see a significant surge related to the current macro situation, and a massive surge in the market this year.

Could it have been in any way forecasted? Not to this level, I argue, no.

Is it justified? I would argue, based on accuracy, that it's not.

In order to understand the negative trends that the company has been seeing, you need to understand the cyclical Spanish economy, which still represents a significant source of earnings. This government is subject to significant spending shifts and political changes, and overall political volatility.

This is not a political article, but an economic one. I will, however, say that I consider Spain to be one of the most volatile EU states to invest in - even more so than some Eastern-European geographies, due to significant unresolved historical legacies, fragmentation, and political tensions. Normally, these wouldn't matter all that much when investing, but here you're investing in a company with significant exposure to politics. If you fail to take this into account, it's like investing in a Southern American company without accounting for politics and FX. Spain was one of Western Europe's longest dictatorships (except for part of Germany, the DDR/GDR), which lasted until 1975, when Gerald Ford was president, and the EU was not a new thing. Let me put it clearly - investing in Spain or a Spanish business is not like investing in, say, a German business.

There are differences that need to be realized, accounted for, and priced for. Just because Indra Sistemas is doing splendidly right now does not mean that this will continue unabated, or that the current valuation is justified (the more important point).

Moving from the "political and historical segway" here back to valuation, I consider a discount of at least 30-50% to be valid here, given the underlying volatility, the lack of an IG rating, the lack of a solid dividend track record, the negative earnings history, and other risks. I validate the company's growth forecast of 10-22% in the next 3-4 years due to certain visibility in defense, but even if it were to grow at 18-20% per year, and even if you were to forecast it at 23-24x P/E, you would not be getting market-beating returns if you invest now.

So, even if you were to ignore the political realities I describe (which I believe are relevant), and if you were to claim a forecast to a 60% higher P/E than historically, with these growth forecasts and ignoring the fact that the company misses negatively more than 60% of the time, this would not even give you 15% annualized at current forecasts. And I believe you, as an investor, should want a lot more than that.

I therefore say any upside the company could rightly expect from military and from good recent results is already included in what the company trades at today. I estimate no more than a 15-20% annualized AEPS growth, but based on historical multiples, as well as a necessity (in my view), of discounting what the company offers here by at least 30-40% due to inherent volatility and the lacks described, I would go no higher than a discounted 15-17x P/E, which comes to a fair value of about €37/share, with a PT of €29/share.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The risks for this company are primarily related to competition for its more commoditized sectors, such as Minsait, while the non-commoditized sectors, such as defense/space, are subject to government funding. As I've pointed out in previous articles on defense contractors, while we're currently seeing a very solid trend for many of these companies, the assumption that this trend is going to last forever or even for a long time is a dangerous one.

Like many other companies with a defense exposure or focus, Indra Sistemas trades at a significant premium to any comparative period. Its current 30x P/E is more than 90% higher than the 20-year average of 15-16x P/E. While current forecasts detail a 20-30% annualized AEPS growth rate, those forecasts come with a historical failure forecast rate of well over 50%, and should not, in my view, be taken "as gospel".

If you instead look at the historical trends, I argue that you see a very worrying historical trend as well as a worrying current valuation trend, which speaks of irrationality, not rationality. Rating this company as a "BUY" (or higher), therefore takes part in that irrationality, and unless you believe either that the irrationality is going to continue, or that there is no irrationality (in which case I'd be interested in that conclusion), you're taking a massive risk here.

Based on my current estimates, I give the company a PT of no higher than €29/share and a fair value of €37/share. I don't believe the earnings are going to turn out as rosy as is currently being estimated.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Indra Sistemas is a good IT/Services/hardware business with an eye towards defense that's been rising for several years now. It has surged significantly during 2025, and if you've invested in the company as of late, it's likely that you're now sitting on very significant gains. However, the gains and the positivity hide an underlying volatility that has been characterizing results for the past 20 years.

Furthermore, the company's operating geography is volatile, and even its civil sectors can't completely immunize it from volatility. The valuation surge is serious enough that I would argue that only a significantly discounted price makes sense. That's the price I also set as my introductory share price here in my first coverage of the company.

I believe that you may, and are likely to, outperform the market over time if you buy here, though your timeframe will have to be longer than I might have originally expected. My price target is €29/share, introduced here in early 2026. The ADR ISMAY comes to a comparative PT of $16.8/share as things stand here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.
